messages.content: bool ourTrueBoy =  true;
The variable "ourTrueBoy" is a boolean type with the value "true".

The new value of "newBoy" is "true". This is because the logical OR operator (||) returns true if either of the operands is true. In this case, "ourTrueBoy" is true, so the expression evaluates to true.

The value of "newBoy" is "false". This is because the logical NOT operator (!) returns the opposite boolean value of the operand. In this case, "ourTrueBoy" is true, so applying the NOT operator to it will give us false.
What type of collection has flexible size, meaning you can add and remove indices from it after it has been initialized?
The type of collection that has a flexible size and allows adding and removing indices after it has been initialized is called a List or an ArrayList (depending on the programming language).
True or false: PlayerPrefs variables store information from Unity scripts on the computer so that the information can be recalled later, and are not destroyed when playback stops.

True. PlayerPrefs variables in Unity store information from scripts on the computer so that the information can be recalled later. They are not destroyed when playback stops and persist across different sessions.

The value of "newBoy" is "false". This is because the logical AND operator (&&) returns true only if both operands are true. In this case, "ourTrueBoy" is true, but "ourFalseBoy" is false, so the expression evaluates to false.

If the code is run and the value of "power" is 20, the following will be printed:
"So powerful!"

This is because the condition `power > 10` is true since 20 is greater than 10. Therefore, the block of code within the if statement will be executed, and "So powerful!" will be printed to the console using Unity's Debug.Log().

True. This code will produce an infinite loop because the condition `true` in the while loop is always true. As a result, the code within the while loop will continue to execute indefinitely, repeatedly printing "This is fine." to the console (assuming the code is executed within a Unity environment with access to the Debug.Log() function).

The following values will be printed by the code:

0
1
2

The code initializes `i` to 0. Then, in each iteration of the while loop, it checks if `i` is less than 3. If it is, it executes the code block inside the loop, which logs the current value of `i`. Afterward, `i` is incremented by 1. This process continues until `i` reaches 3, at which point the condition `i < 3` becomes false, terminating the loop. Therefore, the values 0, 1, and 2 will be printed.

The value of `i` after running this code will be 3.

The code initializes `i` to 0 and then enters an infinite loop using `while (true)`. Inside the loop, it logs the current value of `i` and increments it by 1 using `i++`. After that, it checks if `i` is greater than or equal to 3 using the condition `if (i >= 3)`. If the condition is true, the `break` statement is executed, which terminates the loop and jumps to the code after the loop.

In this case, when `i` reaches the value of 3, the condition `i >= 3` becomes true, and the loop is terminated by the `break` statement. Therefore, the final value of `i` will be 3.
